Front: React concept #1: What is a key idea behind React component design?
Back: React favors small, composable components that encapsulate UI and behavior behind clear props.
Why: This separation of concerns makes features easier to test, reuse, and evolve without breaking unrelated screens.

Front: What problem does the Factory pattern solve?
Back: Factory centralizes object creation so callers depend on abstractions instead of concrete construction details.
Why: This supports substitution, testability, and cleaner separation between usage and instantiation.

Front: What problem does the Strategy pattern solve?
Back: Strategy defines interchangeable algorithms behind a shared interface so behavior can vary at runtime.
Why: It removes large conditionals and keeps algorithm-specific logic isolated and testable.

Front: What problem does the Observer pattern solve?
Back: Observer enables one-to-many notification so dependents react automatically when a subject changes state.
Why: It decouples publishers from subscribers and is common in event-driven and UI architectures.

Front: What problem does the Decorator pattern solve?
Back: Decorator adds behavior to an object dynamically by wrapping it, without changing the original class.
Why: It avoids class explosion from inheritance while enabling composable feature layering.

Front: What problem does the Adapter pattern solve?
Back: Adapter converts one interface into another so incompatible components can work together.
Why: It is useful for integrating legacy code or third-party libraries without invasive refactors.

Front: What problem does the Singleton pattern solve?
Back: Singleton enforces a single shared instance with a global access point for process-wide stateful services.
Why: It can simplify shared coordination but should be used carefully to avoid hidden coupling and test pain.

Front: What problem does the Facade pattern solve?
Back: Facade provides a simplified high-level interface over a complex subsystem.
Why: It lowers cognitive load for callers and localizes subsystem complexity behind one entry point.

Front: What problem does the Builder pattern solve?
Back: Builder constructs complex objects step by step, especially when many optional fields or validation rules exist.
Why: It improves readability and prevents invalid partially-constructed object states.

Front: What problem does the Prototype pattern solve?
Back: Prototype creates new objects by cloning existing configured instances instead of constructing from scratch.
Why: It is useful when setup is expensive or when many similar objects are needed quickly.

Front: What problem does the Command pattern solve?
Back: Command encapsulates a request as an object so actions can be queued, logged, retried, or undone.
Why: It decouples invokers from receivers and supports extensible workflow orchestration.

Back: Factory centralizes object creation so callers depend on abstractions instead of concrete construction details.
Why: This supports substitution, testability, and cleaner separation between usage and instantiation.

Why: It decouples invokers from receivers and supports extensible workflow orchestration.

Front: What problem does the Factory pattern solve?
Back: Factory centralizes object creation so callers depend on abstractions instead of concrete construction details.
Why: This supports substitution, testability, and cleaner separation between usage and instantiation.

Front: What problem does the Strategy pattern solve?
Back: Strategy defines interchangeable algorithms behind a shared interface so behavior can vary at runtime.
Why: It removes large conditionals and keeps algorithm-specific logic isolated and testable.

Front: What problem does the Observer pattern solve?
Back: Observer enables one-to-many notification so dependents react automatically when a subject changes state.
Why: It decouples publishers from subscribers and is common in event-driven and UI architectures.

Front: What problem does the Decorator pattern solve?
Back: Decorator adds behavior to an object dynamically by wrapping it, without changing the original class.
Why: It avoids class explosion from inheritance while enabling composable feature layering.

Front: What problem does the Adapter pattern solve?
Back: Adapter converts one interface into another so incompatible components can work together.
Why: It is useful for integrating legacy code or third-party libraries without invasive refactors.

Front: What problem does the Singleton pattern solve?
Back: Singleton enforces a single shared instance with a global access point for process-wide stateful services.
Why: It can simplify shared coordination but should be used carefully to avoid hidden coupling and test pain.

Front: What problem does the Facade pattern solve?
Back: Facade provides a simplified high-level interface over a complex subsystem.
Why: It lowers cognitive load for callers and localizes subsystem complexity behind one entry point.

Front: What problem does the Builder pattern solve?
Back: Builder constructs complex objects step by step, especially when many optional fields or validation rules exist.
Why: It improves readability and prevents invalid partially-constructed object states.

Front: What problem does the Prototype pattern solve?
Back: Prototype creates new objects by cloning existing configured instances instead of constructing from scratch.
Why: It is useful when setup is expensive or when many similar objects are needed quickly.

Why: It enables safer evolution, controlled migrations, and clearer compatibility guarantees.

Front: AWS concept #1: What is S3 used for?
Back: Amazon S3 provides highly durable object storage for files, backups, static assets, and data lakes.
Why: Its lifecycle, versioning, and access controls make it a foundational building block in cloud architectures.

Front: AWS concept #2: What is DynamoDB used for?
Back: DynamoDB is commonly used for high-scale workloads that need single-digit millisecond reads/writes and flexible schemas.
Why: Its partition model and managed scaling fit event data, session stores, carts, and metadata services.

Front: AWS concept #3: What is SQS used for?
Back: Amazon SQS is a managed message queue for decoupling producers and consumers with durable asynchronous delivery.
Why: Queues smooth traffic spikes, increase resilience, and enable retry and dead-letter handling patterns.

Front: AWS concept #4: What is API Gateway used for?
Back: An API gateway is the single entry point that routes requests to backend services and enforces cross-cutting policies.
Why: It centralizes concerns like auth, rate limits, request shaping, and observability so services stay focused on business logic.

Front: AWS concept #5: What is CloudFront used for?
Back: Amazon CloudFront is a CDN that caches and serves content from edge locations close to users.
Why: It lowers latency, reduces origin load, and adds protections such as TLS termination and DDoS integration.

Front: AWS concept #6: What is Lambda used for?
Back: AWS Lambda runs code on demand without server management, charging per invocation and execution duration.
Why: It is useful for event-driven workflows, background jobs, and bursty workloads with minimal ops overhead.

Front: AWS concept #7: What is EC2 used for?
Back: Amazon EC2 provides resizable virtual compute instances to run applications with flexible CPU, memory, and networking profiles.
Why: It supports custom runtime control while integrating with autoscaling and cloud networking primitives.

Front: AWS concept #8: What is RDS used for?
Back: Amazon RDS is a managed relational database service that automates provisioning, patching, backups, and failover.
Why: It provides SQL databases with strong operational guardrails so teams can focus on schema and query design.

Why: Its lifecycle, versioning, and access controls make it a foundational building block in cloud architectures.

Front: AWS concept #42: What is DynamoDB used for?
Back: DynamoDB is commonly used for high-scale workloads that need single-digit millisecond reads/writes and flexible schemas.
Why: Its partition model and managed scaling fit event data, session stores, carts, and metadata services.

Front: AWS concept #43: What is SQS used for?
Back: Amazon SQS is a managed message queue for decoupling producers and consumers with durable asynchronous delivery.
Why: Queues smooth traffic spikes, increase resilience, and enable retry and dead-letter handling patterns.

Front: AWS concept #44: What is API Gateway used for?
Back: An API gateway is the single entry point that routes requests to backend services and enforces cross-cutting policies.
Why: It centralizes concerns like auth, rate limits, request shaping, and observability so services stay focused on business logic.

Front: AWS concept #45: What is CloudFront used for?
Back: Amazon CloudFront is a CDN that caches and serves content from edge locations close to users.
Why: It lowers latency, reduces origin load, and adds protections such as TLS termination and DDoS integration.

Front: AWS concept #46: What is Lambda used for?
Back: AWS Lambda runs code on demand without server management, charging per invocation and execution duration.
Why: It is useful for event-driven workflows, background jobs, and bursty workloads with minimal ops overhead.

Front: AWS concept #47: What is EC2 used for?
Back: Amazon EC2 provides resizable virtual compute instances to run applications with flexible CPU, memory, and networking profiles.
Why: It supports custom runtime control while integrating with autoscaling and cloud networking primitives.

Front: AWS concept #48: What is RDS used for?
Back: Amazon RDS is a managed relational database service that automates provisioning, patching, backups, and failover.
Why: It provides SQL databases with strong operational guardrails so teams can focus on schema and query design.

Front: CSS concept #1: What is CSS specificity?
Back: Specificity is the browser scoring system that decides which competing CSS selector wins for an element.
Why: Understanding it prevents brittle overrides and reduces styling regressions in large codebases.

Front: System design concept #1: Why is scalability important in large systems?
Back: Scalability lets systems handle growth in users and traffic without unacceptable latency or failure rates.
Why: It protects user experience and avoids expensive emergency rewrites as demand increases.

Front: System design concept #2: Why is latency important in large systems?
Back: Latency directly impacts user experience and conversion, especially on interactive paths.
Why: Low and predictable latency also prevents cascading timeout failures across dependent services.

Front: System design concept #3: Why is load balancing important in large systems?
Back: Load balancing distributes traffic across healthy instances so no single node becomes a bottleneck.
Why: It improves throughput, failover behavior, and operational safety during deployments.

Front: System design concept #4: Why is caching important in large systems?
Back: Caching cuts repeated expensive work and keeps response times stable under heavy read traffic.
Why: It also reduces pressure on databases and downstream dependencies, improving cost and availability.

Front: System design concept #5: Why is rate limiting important in large systems?
Back: Rate limiting protects services from abuse and accidental overload by bounding request volume.
Why: It preserves fairness and keeps critical paths responsive during spikes.

Front: System design concept #6: Why is fault tolerance important in large systems?
Back: Fault tolerance keeps services operating despite partial failures in hardware, networks, or dependencies.
Why: Designing for failure reduces downtime and limits blast radius during incidents.

Front: System design concept #7: Why is stateless design important in large systems?
Back: Stateless services let any instance handle any request because session state is externalized.
Why: That simplifies autoscaling, failover, and rolling deployments in distributed environments.

Front: System design concept #8: Why is microservices important in large systems?
Back: Microservices can improve team autonomy and deployment independence when domains are well-bounded.
Why: The tradeoff is added distributed-system complexity, so the model only helps when scale and org structure justify it.

Front: System design concept #56: Why is microservices important in large systems?
Back: Microservices can improve team autonomy and deployment independence when domains are well-bounded.
Why: The tradeoff is added distributed-system complexity, so the model only helps when scale and org structure justify it.

Front: System design concept #57: Why is scalability important in large systems?
Back: Scalability lets systems handle growth in users and traffic without unacceptable latency or failure rates.
Why: It protects user experience and avoids expensive emergency rewrites as demand increases.

Front: System design concept #58: Why is latency important in large systems?
Back: Latency directly impacts user experience and conversion, especially on interactive paths.
Why: Low and predictable latency also prevents cascading timeout failures across dependent services.

Front: System design concept #59: Why is load balancing important in large systems?
Back: Load balancing distributes traffic across healthy instances so no single node becomes a bottleneck.
Why: It improves throughput, failover behavior, and operational safety during deployments.

Front: System design concept #60: Why is caching important in large systems?
Back: Caching cuts repeated expensive work and keeps response times stable under heavy read traffic.
Why: It also reduces pressure on databases and downstream dependencies, improving cost and availability.

Front: System design concept #61: Why is rate limiting important in large systems?
Back: Rate limiting protects services from abuse and accidental overload by bounding request volume.
Why: It preserves fairness and keeps critical paths responsive during spikes.

Front: System design concept #62: Why is fault tolerance important in large systems?
Back: Fault tolerance keeps services operating despite partial failures in hardware, networks, or dependencies.
Why: Designing for failure reduces downtime and limits blast radius during incidents.

Front: Distributed systems #1: What is CAP theorem?
Back: CAP states that under a network partition, a distributed system must choose between strong consistency and high availability.
Why: It frames practical tradeoffs for replication and consistency design in partition-prone networks.

Front: Distributed systems #2: What is consistent hashing?
Back: Consistent hashing maps keys and nodes onto a ring so adding or removing nodes moves only a subset of keys.
Why: That minimizes rebalancing cost and supports scalable partitioned systems and caches.

Front: Distributed systems #3: What is quorum reads?
Back: Quorum reads require enough replica responses (typically a majority) before returning data.
Why: They trade some latency for stronger consistency guarantees in replicated systems.

Front: Distributed systems #4: What is eventual consistency?
Back: Eventual consistency means replicas may diverge temporarily but converge if no new updates occur.
Why: It improves availability and performance in distributed systems at the cost of short-lived stale reads.

Front: Distributed systems #5: What is leader election?
Back: Leader election is the process a cluster uses to choose one node to coordinate writes or shared decisions.
Why: A stable leader simplifies coordination, ordering, and conflict resolution in distributed systems.

Front: Distributed systems #6: What is replication?
Back: Replication keeps copies of data across multiple nodes for durability, availability, and read scaling.
Why: Replica strategy affects consistency guarantees, failover behavior, and operational complexity.

Front: Why use NoSQL vs relational databases?
Back: NoSQL is often chosen for flexible schemas and horizontal scale, while relational databases excel at joins, constraints, and ACID transactions.
Why: The right choice depends on data model, consistency needs, query patterns, and team operational maturity.

Front: What causes slow SQL queries?
Back: Slow SQL often comes from missing indexes, non-selective predicates, large scans, expensive joins, or poor execution plans.
Why: Using EXPLAIN plans, correct indexing, and query/schema tuning usually yields the biggest performance gains.

Front: What is normalization?
Back: Normalization organizes relational data into well-structured tables to reduce redundancy and update anomalies.
Why: It improves integrity and consistency, though practical designs sometimes denormalize for read performance.

Front: What is over-normalization?
Back: Over-normalization occurs when decomposition creates too many joins and harms common query performance.
Why: Balanced schema design keeps integrity benefits while respecting real access patterns.

Front: What is an index in a database?
Back: A database index is a separate data structure that accelerates lookups and ordering for selected columns.
Why: Proper indexing can transform query performance, but extra indexes also add write and storage cost.

Back: Slow SQL often comes from missing indexes, non-selective predicates, large scans, expensive joins, or poor execution plans.
Why: Using EXPLAIN plans, correct indexing, and query/schema tuning usually yields the biggest performance gains.

Front: What is normalization?
Back: Normalization organizes relational data into well-structured tables to reduce redundancy and update anomalies.
Why: It improves integrity and consistency, though practical designs sometimes denormalize for read performance.

Front: What is over-normalization?
Back: Over-normalization occurs when decomposition creates too many joins and harms common query performance.
Why: Balanced schema design keeps integrity benefits while respecting real access patterns.

Front: What is an index in a database?
Back: A database index is a separate data structure that accelerates lookups and ordering for selected columns.
Why: Proper indexing can transform query performance, but extra indexes also add write and storage cost.

Front: Why use NoSQL vs relational databases?
Back: NoSQL is often chosen for flexible schemas and horizontal scale, while relational databases excel at joins, constraints, and ACID transactions.
Why: The right choice depends on data model, consistency needs, query patterns, and team operational maturity.
